content.text: Right-sizing is the process of matching the AWS resource type and size to the actual workload requirements, measured from real utilisation data rather than estimated at provisioning time. A resource is correctly sized when it meets the performance requirement at the lowest available cost — neither over-provisioned with idle capacity nor under-provisioned and starved under load.

Cost savings in the cloud come from two distinct levers: paying less per unit (through discounts and commitments), and consuming fewer units (through right-sizing and efficiency). The second lever is both larger and more sustainable — you cannot discount your way out of waste.

attrs.alt: Cost savings waterfall showing On-Demand baseline $12,000 reduced to $3,796 through sequential application of rightsizing, Savings Plans, Spot, storage tiering, and transfer optimisation
attrs.title: Cost Savings Waterfall — Stacking Optimisation Levers

content.text: Organisations consistently over-provision for structural reasons that are rational at the time of provisioning but become expensive in steady-state operation. Understanding these causes is prerequisite to eliminating the behaviour.
Performance failures are visible and embarrassing; idle resources are invisible. Engineering teams add safety margins that compound across every service in an architecture.
Migrated workloads are often sized to match the on-premises server spec, which was itself over-provisioned. The migration preserves the inefficiency.
When multiple applications share an instance, teams provision for the aggregate peak of all tenants, which almost never coincides.
When cloud costs land in a central IT budget rather than team P&Ls, individual engineers have no incentive to right-size their resources.
Resources upsized for a launch-day traffic spike are rarely downsized after traffic normalises, because the launch team has moved on to the next project.

Rightsizing is a five-step cycle that repeats continuously as workloads evolve. Run it quarterly at minimum — instance families evolve and a right-sized choice from two years ago may no longer be optimal.
Enable CloudWatch detailed monitoring. Collect CPU, memory (via CloudWatch Agent), network, and disk metrics over 14–30 days to include weekly patterns.
Determine whether the workload is CPU-bound, memory-bound, I/O-bound, or network-bound. The right instance family differs for each bottleneck.
Target the instance that meets the P95 utilisation peak with 20 % headroom. Use Compute Optimizer recommendations as a starting point.
Run load tests on the candidate instance. Verify p99 latency and error rate meet SLOs under simulated peak load.
Change instance type (requires a stop/start for non-bare-metal), or use Auto Scaling with a new launch template. Monitor closely for 72 hours post-change.

AWS EC2 instance families are optimised for different resource ratios. Choosing the wrong family means paying for resources you cannot use or constraining the one resource your workload actually needs.
Using Compute Optimizer for EC2 Recommendations

AWS Compute Optimizer analyses CloudWatch metrics over 14 days and recommends right-sized instance types. Enable it once per account and it continuously updates recommendations as utilisation patterns change.

# Enable Compute Optimizer
aws compute-optimizer update-enrollment-status --status Active

# Get EC2 recommendations for all instances in the account
aws compute-optimizer get-ec2-instance-recommendations \
  --filters Name=Finding,Values=OVER_PROVISIONED \
  --query 'instanceRecommendations[*].{
    Instance: instanceArn,
    CurrentType: currentInstanceType,
    RecommendedType: recommendationOptions[0].instanceType,
    MonthlySavings: recommendationOptions[0].estimatedMonthlySavings.value,
    PercentSavings: recommendationOptions[0].estimatedMonthlySavings.percentage
  }' \
  --output table

# View utilisation stats for a specific instance
aws compute-optimizer get-ec2-recommendation-projected-metrics \
  --instance-arn arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:123456789012:instance/i-0abc123 \
  --stat Maximum \
  --period 86400 \
  --start-time $(date -d '14 days ago' --iso-8601=seconds) \
  --end-time $(date --iso-8601=seconds)

Static rightsizing matches average load. Auto Scaling matches instantaneous load — the fleet size expands during peaks and contracts during troughs. Combined, they eliminate both permanent over-provisioning and manual intervention during traffic events.
Maintain a target metric (e.g. CPU at 60 %, ALB requests per target at 1,000). AWS automatically adds or removes capacity to hold the target. Simplest to configure and most reliable for web tiers.
Pre-scale for known events (business hours, weekly batch jobs, marketing campaigns). Add capacity before the load arrives rather than after CloudWatch detects it.
ML model analyses historical CloudWatch data to forecast load and pre-scale minutes before the predicted peak. Reduces the latency between demand increase and new instance availability.
Protect instances processing long-running requests (e.g. batch jobs, queue consumers) from scale-in termination using instance scale-in protection during active processing.
Lambda Rightsizing: Memory as the Cost Lever

Lambda is billed on GB-seconds: memory allocation (GB) multiplied by execution duration (seconds). The cost relationship is non-obvious — increasing memory allocation can reduce cost if it proportionally shortens execution duration. The AWS Lambda Power Tuning tool finds the optimal memory setting.

Lambda allocates CPU proportionally to memory. A 1,769 MB function gets one full vCPU. CPU-bound functions running at 128 MB are capped at a fraction of one core, which extends duration far more than halving memory saved.

Open-source Step Functions state machine that invokes your function at N different memory sizes, measures duration and cost, and recommends the optimal setting for cost or speed. Run it against production payloads.

Analyses 14 days of invocation data and recommends the optimal memory setting. Identifies both over-provisioned functions (memory unused) and CPU-constrained functions where more memory would reduce duration.

# Estimate whether increasing Lambda memory from 512 MB to 1024 MB saves money
# Assumption: 512 MB takes 500 ms; 1024 MB takes 200 ms (CPU-bound function)

current_memory_gb  = 0.512
current_duration_s = 0.500
new_memory_gb      = 1.024
new_duration_s     = 0.200
requests_per_month = 5_000_000
price_per_gb_s     = 0.0000166667

current_cost = current_memory_gb * current_duration_s * requests_per_month * price_per_gb_s
new_cost     = new_memory_gb * new_duration_s * requests_per_month * price_per_gb_s

print(f"Current (512 MB): ${current_cost:.2f}/month")
print(f"New (1024 MB):    ${new_cost:.2f}/month")
print(f"Saving:           ${current_cost - new_cost:.2f}/month ({(1 - new_cost/current_cost)*100:.0f}%)")
# Output:
# Current (512 MB): $42.67/month
# New (1024 MB):    $17.07/month
# Saving:           $25.60/month (60%)

EBS volumes are billed on their provisioned size, not bytes written. An 500 GB volume that contains 50 GB of actual data costs the same as a full 500 GB volume. Rightsizing EBS has two dimensions: volume type and provisioned size.

Migrating from io1 to gp3 is one of the highest-ROI EBS changes. gp3 delivers 3,000 IOPS and 125 MB/s throughput at the base rate with no extra charge — enough for most databases. io1 charges $0.065 per provisioned IOPS on top of storage, making it 3–5× more expensive for equivalent IOPS counts that do not require the io2 maximum.
RDS instances accumulate cost even when idle. Because database instances rarely scale down automatically, manual rightsizing reviews are essential every quarter.

Route read-heavy application queries to a read replica, allowing the primary to be downsized. A db.r6g.large primary with a db.r6g.large read replica can sometimes replace a db.r6g.2xlarge primary at lower total cost when reads dominate.

Scales from 0.5 to 128 Aurora Capacity Units in seconds. For databases with unpredictable or intermittent load, Aurora Serverless can deliver 60–80 % savings over provisioned RDS by eliminating idle capacity.
RDS supports stopping instances for up to 7 days (then auto-starts). Dev and staging databases running 168 hours/week can be stopped 112 hours/week, reducing instance cost by 67 %.
For databases performing more than 1 million I/O operations per instance per day, Aurora I/O-Optimised (flat storage rate, no per-I/O charge) becomes cheaper than standard Aurora.

# Stop a non-production RDS instance
aws rds stop-db-instance \
  --db-instance-identifier dev-postgres-01

# Create an EventBridge rule to stop RDS instances on a schedule
aws events put-rule \
  --name StopDevRDS \
  --schedule-expression "cron(0 19 ? * MON-FRI *)" \
  --state ENABLED

# Get Compute Optimizer RDS recommendations
aws compute-optimizer get-rds-database-recommendations \
  --filters Name=Finding,Values=OVER_PROVISIONED \
  --query 'rdsDBRecommendations[*].{
    DB: resourceArn,
    CurrentClass: currentDbInstanceClass,
    Recommended: recommendationOptions[0].dbInstanceClass,
    MonthlySaving: recommendationOptions[0].estimatedMonthlySavings.value
  }' \
  --output table

Spot Instances use spare EC2 capacity at up to 90 % discount versus On-Demand. AWS can reclaim Spot Instances with a 2-minute warning when capacity is needed elsewhere. This makes Spot appropriate only for workloads that can tolerate interruption.
Batch processing, CI/CD build agents, data analytics, machine learning training, rendering, genomics pipelines, and Kubernetes node groups for stateless pods.
Request Spot from multiple instance types and AZs to increase capacity availability. Using 5–10 instance types dramatically reduces interruption frequency.

Mixed instance groups in Auto Scaling Groups: set the minimum capacity as On-Demand (for availability guarantee) and excess capacity as Spot (for cost efficiency). The overall fleet cost drops by 40–60 %.
Use EC2 Instance Metadata Service to poll for the rebalance recommendation and termination notice. Implement checkpoint/resume for long-running jobs so work survives interruption.
Commitment Discounts: Savings Plans and Reserved Instances

After rightsizing, commitment discounts are the most impactful way to reduce per-unit cost for stable workloads. The rule: right-size first, then commit. Buying a commitment on an over-provisioned resource locks in the inefficiency at a discount.
Any size within one instance family + region
Fixed instance family, AZ or region
Specific instance type for 1 or 3 years
Specific instance type for 1 or 3 years

Compute Savings Plans are the recommended starting point because they automatically apply to the highest-discount eligible usage across instance families and regions — you do not need to predict which instance type you will be using in 18 months.

Idle resources — running but doing no useful work — are pure waste. AWS Trusted Advisor and Compute Optimizer identify idle resources automatically; the organisation must build a process to act on the findings.
Trusted Advisor flags instances with < 10 % CPU utilisation and < 5 MB/s network throughput over the past 14 days. These are candidates for termination or downsizing to t3.nano.
state (not attached to any instance) continue to be billed. These accumulate from terminated instances where the volume was not set to delete on termination.
Snapshots are stored incrementally but forgotten snapshots from decommissioned resources accumulate. Tag snapshots with
dates and run a Lambda cleanup job weekly.
Trusted Advisor identifies ALBs and CLBs with zero healthy targets or less than 100 requests per day over 7 days. These are candidates for deletion — ALB base cost is $0.008/LCU-hour.

# Find unattached EBS volumes
aws ec2 describe-volumes \
  --filters Name=status,Values=available \
  --query 'Volumes[*].{ID:VolumeId,Size:Size,Type:VolumeType,Created:CreateTime}' \
  --output table

# Find EC2 instances with < 5% average CPU over last 7 days using CloudWatch
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace AWS/EC2 \
  --metric-name CPUUtilization \
  --dimensions Name=InstanceId,Value=i-0abc123 \
  --start-time $(date -d '7 days ago' --iso-8601=seconds) \
  --end-time $(date --iso-8601=seconds) \
  --period 604800 \
  --statistics Average \
  --query 'Datapoints[0].Average'

S3 Intelligent-Tiering and lifecycle policies move objects to cheaper storage classes automatically based on access frequency. For large data lakes and archive use cases, storage tiering is often the second-largest savings lever after compute rightsizing.

Data transfer costs are often discovered late — they are not obvious during architecture design but appear prominently on bills for high-traffic systems. Architecture patterns that minimise data movement reduce both transfer cost and latency.
CloudFront in front of S3 and EC2:
CloudFront egress ($0.0085/GB) is cheaper than direct EC2 egress ($0.09/GB). For any content served to end users, CloudFront reduces transfer cost by 90 % while also reducing latency.
Keep compute and data in the same AZ:

Cross-AZ transfer costs $0.01/GB each direction. EC2 instances reading from RDS or S3 should be in the same AZ as the data source where possible. Use S3 Gateway Endpoints to eliminate NAT Gateway data processing fees for S3 access.
VPC Gateway Endpoints (free for S3 and DynamoDB):
Traffic to S3 or DynamoDB routed through a VPC Gateway Endpoint bypasses the NAT Gateway entirely. Each GB to S3 via NAT Gateway costs $0.045 in processing fees; via Gateway Endpoint it costs $0.
Add S3 and DynamoDB Gateway Endpoints to every VPC. It takes two minutes in the console and eliminates NAT Gateway processing fees for all S3 and DynamoDB traffic immediately.
Benefits of Right-Sizing and Cost Optimisation
Reduces monthly AWS bill 30–70 % for organisations that have not previously applied systematic rightsizing and commitment purchasing.
Eliminates the idle capacity waste that is structural in on-premises but optional in cloud — infrastructure bill correlates to actual business activity.
Rightsizing often improves performance: a smaller but more appropriately typed instance (e.g. switching from r-family to m-family for a CPU-bound workload) performs better at lower cost.
Savings freed from infrastructure can fund new feature development, accelerating business value delivery.
Database CPU averages are misleading — they may be near zero for hours then spike under batch jobs. Always load-test proposed RDS downsizes before production.
A 3-year Reserved Instance on an over-provisioned instance locks in the inefficiency. Always rightsize first and wait for a stable utilisation baseline before committing.
Using Spot for stateful or latency-sensitive workloads:
Spot interruptions are invisible to end users only if the application is designed for them. Adding Spot to a monolithic web application without instance draining causes user-facing errors.
Compute Optimizer and Cost Explorer recommendations lack context without tags. A recommendation to delete an 'idle' EC2 instance is dangerous if you cannot identify what application owns it.
Applying Savings Plans: A Step-by-Step Example

After rightsizing, the next highest-impact action for most teams is purchasing Compute Savings Plans against their stable compute baseline. The correct approach is to buy the minimum comfortable commitment and layer additional coverage over time as patterns stabilise.

# Step 1: Get the Savings Plan purchase recommendation
# AWS analyses your past 7, 30, or 60 days of usage to suggest a commitment level
aws ce get-savings-plans-purchase-recommendation \
  --savings-plans-type COMPUTE_SP \
  --term-in-years ONE_YEAR \
  --payment-option NO_UPFRONT \
  --lookback-period-in-days THIRTY_DAYS \
  --query 'SavingsPlansPurchaseRecommendation.{
    RecommendedHourlyCommitment:SavingsPlansPurchaseRecommendationSummary.HourlyCommitmentToPurchase,
    EstimatedMonthlySavings:SavingsPlansPurchaseRecommendationSummary.EstimatedMonthlySavingsAmount,
    EstimatedSavingsRate:SavingsPlansPurchaseRecommendationSummary.EstimatedSavingsRate
  }' --output table

# Step 2: View current Savings Plan utilisation
aws ce get-savings-plans-utilization \
  --time-period Start=$(date -d '30 days ago' +%Y-%m-01),End=$(date +%Y-%m-01) \
  --granularity MONTHLY \
  --query 'SavingsPlansUtilizationsByTime[*].{
    Month:TimePeriod.Start,
    Utilization:Utilization.UtilizationPercentage,
    NetSavings:Savings.NetSavings.Amount
  }' --output table

Purchase Savings Plans covering 70–80 % of your stable baseline rather than 100 %. This leaves headroom for unexpected workload changes without stranding commitment coverage. You can add additional plans at any time.
Right-sizing eliminates idle capacity waste — the single largest component of cloud over-spend. Apply it before purchasing any commitment discounts.
AWS Compute Optimizer provides ML-based rightsizing recommendations for EC2, EBS, Lambda, Fargate, and RDS based on real utilisation data — enable it in every account.
The savings optimisation order is: rightsizing → Compute Savings Plans → Spot for interruptible workloads → storage tiering → transfer architecture — each lever stacks on the previous.
Lambda memory allocation controls vCPU — increasing memory can reduce cost for CPU-bound functions by shortening execution time faster than the memory rate increase.
VPC Gateway Endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB are free and eliminate NAT Gateway processing fees for those services instantly — one of the highest-ROI five-minute actions in AWS.
